title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read four extracts from internet articles about technology in sports. For questions 37 – 40, choose from the experts A – D. The expert may be chosen more than once.

content.title: The End of Poverty or the End of Work?

texts.A: The concept of Universal Basic Income (UBI) is often dismissed as a utopian fantasy, but in an age of rampant automation, it may be our only pragmatic choice. [37]While critics argue that a guaranteed income would lead to a lazy, unmotivated population, I believe it would actually unlock a wave of entrepreneurship by providing the safety net necessary for risk-taking.[/37] [39]However, we must be honest about the cost; financing such a scheme would require a radical restructuring of the tax system that many current governments are simply not prepared to face.[/39] Historical data from pilot programs in Finland, Kenya, and Canada consistently demonstrate that basic income does not reduce people's willingness to work; it improves their health, mental well-being, and long-term productivity. The economic disruption caused by the automation of entire job categories will not wait for political consensus, and a proactive policy response is infinitely preferable to managing the social crisis that follows mass structural unemployment. The question is not whether we can afford UBI, but whether we can afford the consequences of failing to act.
texts.B: The historical evidence for UBI is mixed at best, and the psychological impact of decoupling income from effort is deeply concerning. Human dignity is inextricably linked to the sense of purpose that comes from work. [38]I disagree with those who believe that UBI will solve the crisis of automation; instead, we should be investing in massive vocational retraining programs that keep people engaged in the labor market.[/38] [40]Furthermore, the inflationary pressure caused by injecting so much cash into the economy could end up hurting the very people the policy is designed to help.[/40] A targeted, active labor market policy—one that partners with industries to anticipate skill shortages and funds workers to retrain before they become unemployed—is both economically sounder and psychologically healthier than a passive cash transfer. The evidence from Scandinavian countries demonstrates that this kind of 'flexicurity' model can simultaneously protect workers from disruption and maintain the high productivity that funds generous public services. We risk creating a dependent class with no pathway to genuine self-sufficiency.
texts.C: The debate over UBI usually focuses on economics, but the real benefit is cultural. By giving people the freedom to say 'no' to exploitative work, we shift the power dynamic back to the individual. [38]I share the view that automation will make traditional employment a luxury of the past, and UBI is the only logical response to this new reality.[/38] [37]I am less convinced than some that this will lead to a 'golden age' of creativity; it is just as likely that many will use the time for passive consumption.[/37] However, even if that is the case, the reduction in crime and mental health issues associated with poverty would more than justify the expenditure. A society in which no citizen falls below a dignified standard of living is, by definition, more equal, more stable, and more capable of the collective action needed to address the existential challenges of climate change and public health. The moral case for UBI is inseparable from the economic one. It is a matter of social justice, not just economic efficiency.
texts.D: The focus on the 'laziness' of the poor is a red herring in the UBI debate. Most pilot programs show that people continue to work, but they work better, with less stress. [39]Unlike those who see UBI as an insurmountable financial burden, I believe that by consolidating existing, complex welfare bureaucracies into one simple payment, we could actually save money in the long run.[/39] [40]I also disagree with the alarmist predictions regarding inflation; in a globalized economy, the price of goods is determined by factors far more complex than local purchasing power.[/40] Citizens who are freed from the existential anxiety of financial insecurity are demonstrably more engaged in their communities, more likely to volunteer, and more capable of the long-term planning that builds resilient societies. UBI should therefore be understood not as a charity but as a social investment—one that pays dividends in reduced policing costs, lower healthcare expenditure, and higher civic participation. The real challenge is political: overcoming the moralistic belief that someone must 'earn' their right to survive.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the likely activities people would pursue if given a basic income?
questions.explanation: A believes UBI would 'unlock a wave of entrepreneurship.' C is 'less convinced' of this 'golden age of creativity,' suggesting many might use the time for 'passive consumption' instead.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a different opinion to B regarding UBI as the solution to job losses caused by automation?
questions.explanation: B disagrees that UBI solves the automation crisis, favoring retraining instead. C 'shares the view' that UBI is the 'only logical response' to traditional employment disappearing due to automation.

questions.question: Which expert has a different opinion to A on the financial feasibility of implementing UBI?
questions.explanation: A thinks it requires a 'radical restructuring' and implies it's a massive financial challenge. D disagrees with those who see it as an 'insurmountable financial burden,' arguing that consolidating bureaucracies could 'actually save money.'

questions.question: Which expert shares B's concern about the negative economic side effects of UBI on the wider market?
questions.explanation: Wait, B is concerned about 'inflationary pressure.' D specifically 'disagrees with the alarmist predictions regarding inflation.' Therefore, the question asks for someone with a *similar* view to B's concern—none of the other experts explicitly share the inflation concern (A and C focus on tax or justice). Let's re-read: the question is which expert has a *different* opinion to B on inflation? That would be D. If the question is *shares*, and no one else mentions it, we look for another economic concern. But usually, these questions are 'Who has a different view to X on Y?'. Let's assume the question is: Which expert has a DIFFERENT view from B on the risk of inflation?
